cataloging_subject.label: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English stanzaic dialogue commonly called the Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard. The subject is one intellectual work represented by six witnesses in the current DIMEV census. It excludes those physical witnesses, Kribel's 1885 parallel-text edition and Latin comparison, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: By the late fourteenth century; exact composition date unknown
catalog_summary.region: England; Kribel tentatively reconstructed a southern Midlands dialect near Kent, but surviving witness dialects are geographically diverse

titles.value: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title following modern scholarship; no single authorial title is established across all six witnesses.

titles.value: Lewd men be not lered in lore / As clerks been in holy writ

assertion.notes: Normalized from DIMEV's name field; witness spellings differ.

titles.value: Her is a gret lamentacion be twene vr ladi & seint Bernard / Of cristes passion hire dere sone þat was so pyneful & so hard

scope.description: Rubric and rhyming heading in the Vernon witness

titles.value: Lamentacio sancti Bernardi de compassione beate Marie virginis ex dulcissimi filii sui passione et eiusdem crudeli morte

scope.description: Rubric in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1

titles.value: Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae [Englische Studien 8 (1885)]
notes: This names Kribel's article and should not be mistaken for a medieval authorial title.

agents.name: Gustav Kribel
notes: Editor of the 1885 parallel transcription immediately encoded by U-M.

agents.name: Richard Rolle
notes: A historical catalogue conjecture rejected by Kribel because the reconstructed poem dialect is incompatible with Rolle's Yorkshire language.

agents.name: Richard Maidstone
notes: Attributed by Horstmann in 1892 without an argument accepted by the current DIMEV record.

assertion.notes: The current DIMEV record supplies no named author, and Kribel concludes that no evidence remains to identify one.
Recorded as reception history, not accepted authorship. The 1856 catalogue conjectured Rolle from the hermit stanza; Kribel rejects the conjecture.
Horstmann asserts the attribution while citing Kribel, but Kribel names no alternative author and DIMEV does not repeat Maidstone's name.

scope.description: The two-witness parallel edition and Latin comparison represented by CME00007

date_statements.display: By the late fourteenth century; exact date unknown

assertion.notes: Late-fourteenth-century copies establish a terminus ante quem; the lower bound is deliberately broad.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth century; Trinity College Oxford MS 57 was copied after 1375, while the Vernon and Laud witnesses also date to the late fourteenth century

assertion.notes: This identifies an earliest surviving horizon, not necessarily one uniquely earliest codex.

places.label: Southern Midlands near Kent
notes: Kribel's historical reconstruction from rhymes shared by his Vernon and Cambridge witnesses; it is not a modern LALME localization.

scope.description: Kribel's reconstructed language of the shared earlier text, not the surface dialect of any one surviving copy

assertion.notes: DIMEV/LALME localizes individual witness hands to Worcestershire, Essex, Sussex, Ely, and Warwickshire; those localizations must not be averaged into a work place.
Kribel's complete Latin comparison stream is an edition layer rather than a co-primary language of the Middle English work.

form_statements.description: Four-stress lines in eight-line stanzas rhyming abababab; witness length and stanza count vary.

summaries.value: A stanzaic Middle English devotional poem in which Saint Bernard asks the Virgin Mary to recount Christ's Passion. Mary narrates arrest, trial, crucifixion, descent from the cross, burial, and her own grief; the dialogue teaches readers compassionate meditation on Christ's suffering through Mary's testimony.

CME00007.xml does not encode three medieval versions. It serializes Kribel's two parallel Middle English witness transcriptions as consecutive divisions and then serializes his page-foot Latin source comparison as a third 'version'. The Latin comparison and German notes belong to the 1885 edition layer.

extent.description: One poem whose extent varies by witness. Kribel prints 91 stanza groups and 736 lines for Vernon (A), and 96 stanza groups and 764 lines for Cambridge Dd.1.1 (B). The repository XML therefore contains 187 LG and 1,500 L elements across two parallel witness streams, not a 1,500-line authored work; its additional Latin division contains one prose stream.

content_parts.label: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
content_parts.summary: The complete dialogue as transmitted with variant ordering, additions, omissions, and endings across six witnesses.

locators.value: ff. 21r-29v; manuscript pages 29-42 in the 1856 catalogue

notes: The six witness texts are versions of this one work, not intrinsic sequential parts.

related_works.title: Quis dabit (Planctus Mariae), attributed to Oglerius of Tridino
notes: Modern scholarship places the poem in the pseudo-Bernard Quis dabit tradition. Snow, following C. W. Marx, reports an Anglo-Norman poetic intermediary; Kribel instead compared the poem directly with the Latin pseudo-Bernard sermon. The exact route remains an open question.

related_works.title: Father and Son and Holy Ghost / Almighty God in Trinity
notes: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 retains this as a separate but related record; do not merge its three witnesses into record-3066's six-witness census.

related_works.title: Cursor Mundi Lamentation of Mary
notes: A distinct related version in the wider Quis dabit tradition.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00007.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00007 and preserves Kribel's A and B Middle English witness streams, Latin comparison, notes, page milestones, italic expansions, and a 2016 restructuring review.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00007.
evidence.locator: item title, responsibility display, rights, contents, and entire-text link
evidence.summary: Current item page identifies the Kribel title and 1885 source, exposes A, B, and a third version division, and asserts public-domain / No Copyright-United States status. Its display's inclusion of Richard Rolle as an author is not accepted as work-level authorship.

evidence.citation: Gustav Kribel, 'Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae,' Englische Studien 8 (1885), 67-114.
evidence.locator: article pp. 67-114; edited parallel texts and Latin comparison pp. 85-114; PDF pages 77-124
evidence.summary: Immediate source edition: describes Vernon and CUL Dd.1.1, compares their texts and sources, reconstructs a southern-Midland dialect near Kent, rejects Rolle authorship, explains a parallel non-normalized transcription with italic abbreviation expansions, and prints a Latin comparison based on an Antwerp 1616 edition.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript), complete Digital Bodleian IIIF facsimile.
evidence.summary: Official metadata dates the manuscript 1381-1400 (c. 1390), and the complete color surrogate maps the poem's opening and ending to exact canvases within the full codex.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. 1 (Cambridge: University Press, 1856), 1-6, especially p. 2.
evidence.locator: Dd.1.1 entry pp. 1-6; poem at printed p. 2 / PDF p. 24; manuscript pp. 29-42
evidence.summary: Describes Dd.1.1 as a mutilated, principally parchment codex of the latter half of the fourteenth century; gives the poem's rubric, incipit, explicit, manuscript-page range, Latin-source claim, hermit stanza, and historical Rolle conjecture.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1, record-3066 (2026).
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml, record-3066; data/Manuscripts.xml entries for six witnesses
evidence.summary: Current six-witness census; supplies incipit title, dialogue description, eight-line abababab form, subjects, exact folio ranges, witness incipits/explicits and rubrics, IMEV/NIMEV 1869, MED BIB857, witness dialect references, and related DIMEV records 1275 and 5020.

evidence.citation: Richard Gameson, The Medieval Manuscripts of Trinity College, Oxford: A Descriptive Catalogue (2018), MS 57.
evidence.locator: MS 57 description, pp. 320 ff.; poem at ff. 167r-169v
evidence.summary: Dates the southern-English parchment codex to the last quarter of the fourteenth century after 1375, describes its 169 leaves and contents, and identifies the final Lamentacio Marie et Bernardi as ending imperfectly at the codex boundary.
notes: The exact PDF was indexed and readable in search results, but an automated direct download encountered the site's JavaScript proof-of-work challenge; no local copy is claimed.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 11307, record 032-002109064.
evidence.locator: whole-record title, scope and content, date, access, and blank digitised-content field
evidence.summary: Describes a fifteenth-century vellum manuscript containing a four-line-stanza dialogue between Saint Bernard and the Virgin with facing recent transcripts by Joseph Haslewood; the current record exposes no digitised-content link.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1, EETS Original Series 98 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1892), 297-328.
evidence.summary: Later Vernon-based edition with variants from CUL Dd.1.1, incomplete Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and an unresolved 'MS. L. 70'; asserts Richard Maidstone authorship but supplies no argument accepted by DIMEV.

evidence.citation: Clare Marie Snow, Maria Mediatrix: Mediating the Divine in the Devotional Literature of Late Medieval and Early Modern England (PhD thesis, University of Toronto, 2012).
evidence.locator: chapter 3, especially pp. 131-152 and p. 134 witness/source discussion
evidence.summary: Places the poem in affective Marian devotion, lists the same six IMEV 1869 witnesses, and, following C. W. Marx, reports an Anglo-Norman poetic Quis dabit intermediary and an added prologue in the Vernon form.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XPath and element-count inspection of CME00007.xml.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY/DIV1 and its three DIV2 children
evidence.summary: Counts 187 LG, 1,500 L, 3 P, 89 PB, 49 NOTE, 819 HI, and 356 MILESTONE elements. The A division has 91 LG/736 L; B has 96 LG/764 L; the third division is Kribel's Latin prose comparison.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition date and dialect region be established more precisely from all six witnesses?
next_steps: Consult C. W. Marx's full 1990 chapter and later linguistic scholarship comparing all witnesses.
Keep work origin distinct from LALME localizations of individual copyists.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin and/or Anglo-Norman textual route underlies this Middle English version?
next_steps: Consult Marx's 1990 and 1994 studies and compare their source analysis with Kribel's Antwerp-1616 collation.
Do not treat Kribel's early-modern Latin printing as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: What evidence, if any, led Horstmann to attribute the poem to Richard Maidstone?
next_steps: Review Horstmann's unpublished notes or later attribution scholarship.
Continue to catalog the work as anonymous unless reliable evidence supersedes DIMEV and Kribel.

notes: DIMEV's six-witness census supersedes Kribel's statement that the poem survives in only two manuscripts; Kribel's two are the only direct Middle English sources of CME00007.
Kribel's lost common exemplar is a stemmatic inference for Vernon and CUL Dd.1.1, not a surviving manuscript entity or a license to infer a single canonical line count.
Rights for the medieval work, nineteenth-century editions, U-M encoding, modern scans, and manuscript photographs are kept separate in the lineage manifest.
